literal: the original substrate, the natural state; from pra- + √kṛ 'to make' (literally 'that from which makings proceed')
invariant_definition: The material principle out of which the manifest world emerges. The category enters Vedānta from Sāṅkhya, where *prakṛti* is one of two ultimate principles (*puruṣa* the conscious witness, *prakṛti* the unconscious productive matter constituted by the three *guṇas*: *sattva*, *rajas*, *tamas*). Vedānta universally refuses Sāṅkhya's strict dualism and re-locates *prakṛti* under *brahman*, but the schools differ on how. All Vedāntic schools accept that *prakṛti* is constituted by the three *guṇas*; that the *jīva* in *saṃsāra* is associated with *prakṛti* through embodiment; that *prakṛti* is not the conscious principle. They differ on whether *prakṛti* is real (Viśiṣṭādvaita, Dvaita, Vaiṣṇava bhedābheda) or *vyāvahārika* (Advaita), and on whether it is identical with *māyā* (Advaita) or distinct from it (Vaiṣṇava schools that distinguish *prakṛti* as material from *māyā* as Lord's veiling power).

per_school.definition: *Prakṛti* is functionally identified with *māyā* in many contexts; it is the material principle as it appears under the *vyāvahārika* standpoint, but is *anirvacanīya*. Sāṅkhya's *pradhāna* — *prakṛti* as independent ultimate — is refuted at length in *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 2.2.1ff.

*Prakṛti* is real, eternal, and is one of the three *tattvas* under *acit* (the unconscious side of *brahman*'s body). It really transforms (*pariṇāma*) into the manifest world; the world is therefore real, not apparent.

*Prakṛti* is real, eternal, and one of Viṣṇu's *paratantra* (dependent) realities, distinct from *jīvas* and from Viṣṇu himself (one of the *pañca-bheda*: matter–matter difference, matter–jīva difference, matter–Viṣṇu difference). Its transformation into the world is real, governed by Viṣṇu's will.

*Prakṛti* is the *guṇa-māyā* portion of Kṛṣṇa's *bahiraṅgā-śakti* — Kṛṣṇa's external power as material substrate. Distinct from *jīva-māyā* (which veils the *jīva* specifically). Real, but external — outside the eternal *Vaikuṇṭha* lila-substance.

translator_note: 'Nature' is the standard but inadequate translation. *Prakṛti* in technical Sāṅkhya-Vedānta usage is not 'the natural world' (a modern Western category) but the *material principle* — the unconscious productive substance constituted by the three *guṇas*. Modern English 'nature' (= the non-human, the biological, the not-cultural) is alien to the Sanskrit.
